channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: Kinross releases 2025 Sustainability Report
item.description:
Strong ongoing commitment to Sustainability, contributing $4.9 billion in economic benefits through payments to governments, wages, procurement and community support

TORONTO, June 01, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) --

Kinross Gold Corporation (TSX: K; NYSE: KGC) (“Kinross” or the “Company”) is pleased to announce the publication of its 2025 Sustainability Report (the “Report”), providing comprehensive insights into its Sustainability Strategy, priorities, and performance over the past year. The 18th edition of the Report is available at www.kinross.com/2025-Sustainability-Report.

“Mining responsibly is central to how we operate and sustainability is a core part of Kinross’ culture, strategy and plans for future growth,” said J. Paul Rollinson, CEO. “Our values-driven approach guides our Sustainability Strategy, which prioritizes three focus areas: People, Planet and Efficiency. These areas emphasize what’s important to us – the wellbeing of our workforce and communities, protecting the environment, and using energy, water, and land as efficiently as possible. Our 2025 Report provides an update to our stakeholders on the performance and projects that underscore our commitment to Sustainability.”

Kinross maintained strong performance across core sustainability metrics and continued to be recognized in 2025 across Sustainability ratings and rankings. The Company maintained its high standing in the S&P Corporate Sustainability Assessment, remaining in the top 10 of the Mining and Metals Sector. As of May 1, 2026, Kinross has been included in the Dow Jones Best in Class World Index and the Dow Jones Best in Class Index North America. The Company has also been included in the S&P Global Sustainability Yearbook for 2026 for the 13th consecutive year.

Kinross also continued to demonstrate strong governance and adherence to industry-leading standards. The Company was the top scoring mining company in The Globe and Mail’s annual corporate governance ranking. It also maintained conformance for the fifth consecutive year with the Responsible Gold Mining Principles (RGMPs), established by the World Gold Council.

Highlights from the 2025 Sustainability Report include:

People: Workforce and Community

  • Continued to embed the Safeground Health & Safety program across the Company through increased field engagements and training, leadership, standardization and digital transformation. Since 2023, recorded approximately 14,000 Safety Excellence training completions.
  • Launched a new Critical Risk Management solution which addresses 18 Global Core Risks, including 14 for surface mining and four for underground mining.
  • Increased female workforce representation to a high of 15% and returned to 30% female representation on Kinross’ Board of Directors (as of April 30, 2026). Total turnover improved to 9.8%, the best result in the past decade.
  • Generated $4.9 billion in economic benefits to host countries through payments to governments ($722 million), wages ($809 million), procurement ($3.3 billion), and community investments ($19 million). Since 2010, Kinross has contributed $58 billion to the economies of host countries.
  • Demonstrated ongoing commitment to local benefits within host countries, with local employment comprising 99% of the Company’s workforce and approximately 94% of management, and local procurement making up 87% of total procurement.
  • Recorded approximately 1 million beneficiaries from $19 million spent on social investments and community programs, the highest social investment spend to date.

Planet – Nature and Climate

  • Completed the update of the environment management system and nature strategy in line with the guidance from the Task Force on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) framework.
  • Completed a third-party physical climate risk analysis of infrastructure at sites and projects.
  • For the 33rd year, maintained a record of zero tailings breaches and upheld high tailings management standards.
  • Paracatu’s tailings facilities received the top-level AA classification from the Engineer of Record, under Brazil’s National Mining Agency’s recently introduced categories for dam management and monitoring.
  • Achieved a waste recycling rate of 55%, a five-year high, which included organic waste programs at Paracatu and Tasiast, and approximately 20,000 tonnes of steel scrap recycled off-site at Tasiast.

Efficiency – Energy, Water and Land

  • Maintained focus on energy efficiency through the completion of 38 energy efficiency projects, delivering savings of approximately $10.5 million, 12 million litres of fuel, and 8,925 MWh of energy. Together, these projects delivered savings of approximately 35,286 tonnes of CO2e in greenhouse gas emissions (GHG), representing an estimated GHG emissions reduction of 2.4% from 2024 emissions.
  • Recorded a GHG intensity rate of 707 kg CO2/Au. eq. oz., remaining on track to achieve the Company’s 2030 intensity target.
  • Leveraged the low emissions profile of power grids in South America as well as Kinross’ strategic investments in renewables:
    • Renewable sources represented 23% of total energy consumption and 67% of electricity consumed in 2025.
    • At Paracatu, approximately 94% of electricity consumed and 53% of energy consumed was from renewable sources.
    • In Chile, a power purchase agreement provides 100% renewable electricity, representing 37% of total site energy consumption.
    • Approximately 22% of electricity was generated from renewable sources at the Tasiast solar plant, representing 4% of total site energy consumption.
  • Maintained efficient use of water with 75% of water recycled at operating mine sites, and rolled out the Kinross Water Management Standard across sites. In total, 26% of water consumed, representing 18 million m3 of water, was discharged or diverted back to the environment.
  • Reclaimed 94 hectares (“ha”) of land at operating mine sites during 2025. Total cumulative land protected stands at 13,620 ha, representing 64% of land currently disturbed.

Governance

  • Launched the “Mining with Integrity” program to reinforce awareness of Kinross’ Code of Business Conduct and Ethics.
  • Continued engagement with suppliers on sustainability topics including emissions reductions, water resilience, and human rights. Within Kinross’ workforce (technology users), an 89% completion rate for a human rights training course was achieved.
  • Completed a cybersecurity due diligence assessment.
  • Completed crisis management training at Corporate and sites.

The 2025 Report maintains our established practice of alignment with the voluntary standards of the Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB) Mining and Metals Sustainability Accounting Standards, the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards, and the Task Force on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) framework. It also continues our work towards alignment with the European Union’s (EU) Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).

In addition to summarizing the Company’s performance in 2025, the Report also outlines goals for 2026, including:

  • Continued priority focus on health and safety through Safeground;
  • Workforce skills training;
  • Strong community relationships and social investment;
  • Continued comprehensive nature stewardship;
  • Building on existing and new opportunities for further efficiencies in energy, water, and land; and
  • Continued strong governance.

About Kinross Gold Corporation

Kinross is a Canadian-based global senior gold mining company with operations and projects in the United States, Brazil, Mauritania, Chile and Canada. Our focus is on delivering value based on the core principles of responsible mining, operational excellence, disciplined growth, and balance sheet strength. Kinross maintains listings on the Toronto Stock Exchange (symbol: K) and the New York Stock Exchange (symbol: KGC).

Media Contact
Samantha Sheffield
Director, Corporate Communications
phone: 416-365-3034
Samantha.Sheffield@Kinross.com


Investor Relations Contact
David Shaver                                 
Executive Vice-President, Investor Relations & Communications                
phone: 416-365-2854                        
InvestorRelations@Kinross.com

Source: Kinross Gold Corporation

Source: Kinross Gold Corporation

item.title: US SEC moves to roll back climate risk disclosure rules | BusinessGreen News
item.description: Regulator formally proposes plan to rescind 2024 rule requiring listed companies to report on climate-related risks.

item.title: Too many global funders are overlooking women in Latin America - Alliance magazine
item.description: Despite systemic denial of reproductive rights, high levels of gender-based violence, accelerating climate risk, and worsening restrictions on civil ...

item.title: These Indian cities at high risk of heat, rain and flooding as weather turns - India Today
item.description:
These Indian cities at high risk of heat, rain and flooding as weather turns

An Azim Premji University assessment says India's coastal cities face rising heat, heavier rain, flooding and sea-level risks by 2040. The findings point to growing pressure on livelihoods, infrastructure and ecosystems, with urgent adaptation needed.

India's climate is undergoing a profound churn as rising global temperatures reshape weather patterns across the country, and India's coasts are not immune.

India's major coastal cities could face significantly higher risks from flooding, extreme rainfall, rising temperatures and sea-level rise by 2040, according to a new climate assessment of the country's 11,000-kilometre coastline.

The report, released by researchers at Azim Premji University, warns that climate impacts are no longer distant threats but are likely to intensify within the next 15 years, affecting millions of people living in coastal districts.

WHAT IS HAPPENING TO INDIA'S CLIMATE?

Heatwaves in India are becoming longer and more intense, while extreme rainfall events are increasing even in regions that traditionally receive moderate rain.

Cyclones in the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal are growing stronger, glaciers in the Himalayas are retreating, and sea levels are rising along India's vast coastline.

The result is a climate marked by greater unpredictability, long dry spells interrupted by cloudbursts, frequent floods, worsening droughts, and increasing risks to agriculture, infrastructure, ecosystems and millions of livelihoods.

THESE INDIAN COASTAL CITIES AT HIGH RISK

Among the cities identified as highly vulnerable are Mumbai, Chennai, Kochi, Visakhapatnam, Kolkata, Surat, Mangaluru and several urban centres along the coasts of Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu and Kerala.

Mumbai is expected to face a dangerous combination of sea-level rise, storm surges and extreme rainfall events. The city, which already experiences severe monsoon flooding, could see more frequent inundation of low-lying neighbourhoods as higher sea levels reduce the ability of rainwater to drain into the sea.

Chennai and other parts of the Tamil Nadu coast are projected to experience stronger cyclones, heavier rainfall and worsening urban floods. Coastal erosion and saltwater intrusion into freshwater sources are also expected to become more common.

On the eastern coast, cities such as Visakhapatnam and Kakinada in Andhra Pradesh, along with Puri and Paradip in Odisha, could face increasing cyclone intensity and storm surge flooding.

These areas are already among the most cyclone-prone regions in India and are expected to become more vulnerable as ocean temperatures rise.

Kerala's coastal cities, including Kochi and Kozhikode, are projected to experience heavier rainfall events, flooding and accelerating coastal erosion. Scientists also warn of increasing "wet-bulb" temperatures, where high heat combines with humidity to create dangerous conditions for human health.

The report highlights the Sundarbans region and the Bengal coast as some of the most vulnerable areas in the country. Rising sea levels and repeated cyclones could threaten settlements, agriculture and fragile ecosystems. Kolkata and Haldia may face greater flooding risks linked to both coastal and riverine processes.

Meanwhile, cities along the Gujarat coast, including Surat, Bhavnagar and Porbandar, are expected to see rising heat stress, flooding and stronger cyclone impacts from the Arabian Sea.

WHAT WILL BE THE IMPACT ON INDIA?

Researchers say the changing climate could disrupt fisheries, agriculture, tourism, ports and other coastal industries that support millions of livelihoods.

Infrastructure such as roads, power systems, ports and housing may also come under increasing pressure from more frequent extreme weather events.

The report calls for urgent adaptation measures, including improved flood management, climate-resilient infrastructure, coastal ecosystem restoration and better urban planning.

Without action, India's coastal cities could face a future marked by heavier rain, deeper floods and dangerous heat by 2040.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Interview with Ms. Rocío Salazar Arrarte, Executive Vice President, and Mr. Carlos José ...
item.description:
Banco Bolivariano, one of Ecuador’s 10 largest banks, is known for its strong performance, customer-centric mission and dedication to innovation. Launched in 1980 and headquartered in Guayaquil, it has significantly contributed to the country’s financial development by expertly serving individuals, small and medium-sized businesses and corporations, building a reputation for financial strength, prudent risk management and ethical governance.

The bank’s business model is centred on offering comprehensive banking solutions, and it has consistently prioritised technology investments to enhance efficiency, security and overall performance. These initiatives reflect the bank’s commitment to adapting to changing customer expectations.

Banco Bolivariano emphasises personalised service and financial inclusion. Its network of branches and non-banking correspondent service points is supported by teams of experienced professionals who focus on understanding client needs and delivering tailored solutions. The bank also promotes sustainable practices, financial education and social initiatives aimed at fostering positive impacts within the communities it serves.

According to its 2024 Sustainability Report, Banco Bolivariano’s vision is to maintain the bank “as one of the most important financial institutions in Ecuador, preserving an optimal financial position and excellence in service from our staff, with rapid response times and creating new products tailored to the needs of each customer segment”.

International Banker was pleased to discuss the bank’s latest progress in achieving this vision with Executive Vice President Rocío Salazar Arrarte and Sustainability Manager Carlos José Santos.

Thank you both for speaking with me today….

I’d like to start by asking you a few questions, if I may, Ms. Salazar. According to Banco Bolivariano’s published financial statements as of December 2025, the bank achieved solid year-on-year growth of 14.8 percent in total assets. To what do you attribute this growth? And what are the bank’s financial priorities for 2026?

The 14.8-percent growth in total assets reflects Banco Bolivariano’s ability to sustain solid performance in a demanding environment. This result was driven principally by the strong expansion of public deposits, which increased by 15.8 percent, significantly strengthening our funding base. This enabled a 20.5-percent increase in our commercial-loan portfolio, reinforcing our role as a strategic ally to the country’s productive sector. These achievements demonstrate our close alignment with the real needs of our corporate clients and reaffirm our commitment to supporting Ecuador’s sustainable economic development.

Our progress was further supported by the consolidation of our digital-transformation agenda, which deepened client relationships, enhanced operational efficiency and strengthened our business lines’ performance. These advances reflect the firmness of a strategy built on financial prudence, continuous innovation and a deep institutional commitment to service excellence.

Looking ahead to 2026, our priorities include continuing to expand our deposit and loan portfolios, safeguarding profitability through disciplined margin management and reinforcing our presence in strategic segments. We will advance initiatives that strengthen our value proposition, prioritise sustainable growth and consolidate our position as a solid, competitive and trusted financial institution for individuals and businesses alike.

To advance its financial-inclusion objectives, Banco Bolivariano has strengthened its non-banking correspondent network. What do these service points offer the previously underbanked or unbanked?

Non-banking correspondents play a fundamental role in extending access to financial services in areas where traditional branches are not present. Through a network of more than 5,946 service points strategically located across the country, Banco Bolivariano offers over 100 transactional services, including deposits, withdrawals, credit-card payments, cash advances, remittances and utility collections. This network enables individuals in previously underserved or remote communities to conduct their daily financial transactions without travelling to a branch, thereby reducing access barriers and supporting the dynamism of local economies.

From an institutional perspective, this model reflects our commitment to strengthening financial inclusion practically and sustainably. While our current coverage has allowed us to reach a significant portion of the population, we recognise that there remain communities where a greater presence would be beneficial. Our strategy envisions continued expansion into areas that still lack banking services, as well as enhancing our service offering according to users’ evolving needs, reaffirming our dedication to promoting equitable access to financial services and supporting the development of communities throughout Ecuador.

In 2025, Banco Bolivariano cemented its position as a leader in remittances. Of the industry-wide total of 70.4 percent of transfers paid into accounts, 36.4 percent were deposited into Banco Bolivariano accounts. What explains this high level of customer trust?

Banco Bolivariano’s strong position in the remittances segment reflects more than two decades of consistent service, operational reliability and proximity to migrant families. The bank pays over US$600 million in remittances annually, a 20-percent increase over the previous year, benefiting more than 1.2 million Ecuadorian families, a testament to the trust placed in our institution.

Our clients value the speed, security and immediacy of our service. Transfers credited to accounts are available in real-time, 24 hours a day, seven days a week, including weekends and holidays. This reliability, combined with broad national coverage and long-standing partnerships with leading international remittance companies, has consolidated our leadership.

We see remittances as a continued growth area for 2026 and remain committed to expanding alliances, strengthening processes and serving families with the professionalism and empathy they deserve.

Banco Bolivariano facilitates access to credit for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), while integrating sustainability and ESG (environmental, social and governance) considerations into this objective—particularly through its PYME Mujer programme, which supports women-led SMEs. The programme has achieved significant growth, with outstanding balances increasing from $71 million in December 2024 to $101 million in November 2025. To what key factors do you attribute this growth?

Our PYME Mujer portfolio’s strong growth is fundamentally the result of the trust that Ecuador’s entrepreneurs place in Banco Bolivariano and of the institution’s longstanding commitment to accompany the SME sector with professionalism, prudence and genuine proximity. We have strengthened our value proposition for SMEs by modernising processes, expanding our digital channels and improving the agility with which clients can access financial solutions. These efforts have allowed us to serve a larger number of businesses with greater efficiency, clarity and responsiveness.

Our digital transformation has been particularly important in this evolution. By streamlining onboarding, offering self-service tools and enhancing credit-evaluation processes, we have made it easier for SMEs to interact with the bank and obtain financing aligned with their growth objectives. Women-led enterprises have responded positively to this combination of technological agility, institutional solidity and transparent service.

The portfolio’s expansion reflects our clients’ confidence, the bank’s reputation for responsible management and our continued dedication to supporting the development of Ecuador’s productive sector.

Thank you, Ms. Salazar. Turning to you now, Mr. Santos, Banco Bolivariano is widely recognised for its sustainable-finance instruments. What are the key features of the bank’s blue bond and biodiversity bond? How are the proceeds deployed to advance sustainability objectives?

Banco Bolivariano’s leadership in sustainable finance is grounded in our corporate purpose: to drive Ecuador’s sustainable progress with responsibility and passion. This purpose guides every thematic instrument we place in the market and ensures that each transaction contributes meaningfully to national development.

Our blue bond, the first ever issued in Ecuador, represents a milestone in mobilising capital toward the preservation and responsible use of marine and coastal resources. Structured with the support of IDB Invest and FinDev Canada, the US$80-million issuance channels financing toward projects in sustainable aquaculture and fishing, water and wastewater management, solid-waste reduction and circular-economy initiatives. These activities must demonstrate measurable environmental benefits and align with internationally recognised sustainability standards, particularly in sectors such as the sustainable seafood industry. As of year-end 2023, the blue portfolio was expanding more rapidly than initially envisioned, driven by clients with strong environmental certifications.

Banco Bolivariano is advancing Ecuador’s first biodiversity bond, a transaction of up to US$120 million. This issuance originates directly from our internal adoption of the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) framework, which has enabled us to systematically assess our nature-related risks and opportunities. This analytical process highlighted the strategic importance of biodiversity to Ecuador, a country recognised globally as one of the most biodiverse on the planet, and underscored the financial sector’s responsibility to safeguard these natural assets. The biodiversity bond’s proceeds will finance conservation, ecosystem restoration, sustainable agriculture and other nature-positive activities, supported by IDB Invest’s technical assistance to strengthen impact measurement and monitoring systems.

Through these thematic instruments, Banco Bolivariano reaffirms its commitment to aligning financial innovation with the protection of Ecuador’s natural heritage and the creation of long-term sustainable value for the country.

You offer specialised credit products and training programmes to help clients transition to a low-carbon economy. What are the most important changes clients must make?

Beyond providing specialised credit products, Banco Bolivariano’s primary objective is to help clients create added value by integrating sustainability practices that strengthen their efficiency, competitiveness and long-term resilience. Through our training programmes on aquaculture, biodiversity and low-carbon transitions, we guide clients on practical improvements. These measures reduce environmental impact while enhancing market access and operational performance.

Our programmes also help senior decision-makers understand nature-related risks, identify impacts and anticipate emerging sustainability requirements in international markets. This strengthened capacity enables clients to align with global standards and access green-financing opportunities, including resources from our blue bond and the forthcoming biodiversity bond.

In our experience, the most important changes involve improving data collection, adopting resource-efficient technologies and embedding sustainability into internal governance structures.

The bank assesses physical climate risks during property valuations. How is the risk-management process conducted? What are the biggest challenges when working with climate-change scenarios?

The assessment of physical environmental risks forms an integral part of our prudent, forward-looking risk-management framework. During property valuations, our teams apply a formal methodology that incorporates official national datasets and geospatial analysis to determine each asset’s exposure to natural hazards. This enables us to evaluate not only an operation’s financial fundamentals but also its long-term vulnerability and the potential implications for portfolio resilience.

Our approach is consistent with the governance principles established under international disclosure standards, which have strengthened our internal structures, broadened the use of scenario-based analysis and enhanced transparency in identifying environmental risks across the institution. This process allows us to communicate these risks clearly to clients, prepare them to face potential adversities and help them recognise operational improvements and business opportunities that may arise from strengthening their resilience.

One of the principal challenges in working within these scenarios is their evolving nature. Data availability, methodologies and supervisory expectations advance continuously, requiring us to refine assumptions, update analytical tools and invest in expanded technical capabilities. We consider this an essential component of responsible risk management and supporting our clients in building stronger, more adaptable businesses.

What certifications does Banco Bolivariano help clients achieve? What training do employees undergo to ensure they can competently assist clients?

We recognise that many of our clients aim to strengthen their projects’ quality and competitiveness by adopting internationally recognised sustainability certifications. The bank provides guidance on achieving EDGE (Excellence in Design for Greater Efficiencies) and LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) standards—fundamental to advancing efficient building design and responsible resource use. These certifications enable our clients to incorporate best-practice criteria in areas such as energy performance, water optimisation and materials selection—improvements that generate tangible operational benefits and enhance the long-term value of their assets. We are developing new support lines to accompany clients in obtaining biodiversity-related certifications, reflecting our commitment to evolving alongside emerging global requirements.

The bank relies on a specialised team that includes professionals with expertise in green building, environmental management and biodiversity. We ensure that our commercial officers and other frontline teams undergo continuous training on certification requirements, environmental-risk considerations and sustainable-project criteria. This preparation allows them to guide clients with clarity, diligence and a deep sense of responsibility, reinforcing our institution’s commitment to fostering sustainable and resilient business development.

The learning ecosystem is a strategic training platform for corporate clients. How does this platform help them achieve sustainability objectives?

The learning ecosystem is a strategic instrument for strengthening our corporate clients’ capabilities, particularly in sustainability, offering structured training programmes that help organisations deepen their understanding of environmental and social considerations, as well as the governance practices required to operate responsibly in an increasingly demanding business environment.

The ecosystem provides access to specialised content, practical tools and expert-led sessions that enable clients to incorporate sustainability criteria into their operations tangibly and measurably. Topics range from efficient resource management and responsible production practices to identifying nature-related risks and opportunities. By strengthening this knowledge base, clients are better equipped to adopt international standards, improve internal processes and advance toward formal sustainability objectives, including certifications and enhanced disclosure practices.

The platform reinforces our role as a trusted partner in our clients’ development. It allows us to accompany them with rigour and clarity, ensuring they are prepared to compete in markets where sustainability is increasingly synonymous with resilience, quality and long-term growth.

How does the bank ensure that excellent staff service is maintained?

Our service excellence is sustained by a culture of professionalism, integrity and technical competence. This begins with rigorous selection and is reinforced through continuous training via our learning ecosystem, covering customer service, operational standards, regulatory requirements and responsible technology use.

Service quality is also supported by strong governance: clear processes, defined responsibilities and ongoing leadership oversight across all channels and segments. Finally, structured feedback helps us continuously improve in line with evolving client needs.

Maintaining excellence is not a single initiative but a disciplined practice, one grounded in training, governance and a deep commitment to the communities we serve.

Banco Bolivariano certainly maintains excellence. Thank you both again for your time today.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Heatwaves and the Role of Geneva
item.description:
Update

Heatwaves and the Role of Geneva

Last updated: 01 Jun 2026

Heatwaves - prolonged periods of unusually high temperatures - have become more frequent, intense and long-lasting in many regions around the world. This page provides relevant information, data and resources from leading institutions and partners in Geneva and across the globe.

About Heatwaves

Heatwave – a period where local excess heat accumulates over a sequence of unusually hot days and nights.

A heatwave consists, according to the World Meteorological Organization, of a period of abnormally hot weather that can last from a few days to months where the maximum and minimum temperatures are unusually high to a location. The minimum temperature is as important as the maximum temperature as cooler nights allow the body to recover and if the nights are unusually hot, higher temperatures will be reached earlier in the day and last for longer.

Heatwaves amplify many risks, such as health-related or economic risks, including increased human mortality, drought and water quality, wildfire and smoke, power shortages and agricultural losses.

In the past years, heatwaves have become a more common phenomenon, spreading rapidly into new regions of the globe and occurring at unseasonable times of the year. As early as 2004, researchers predicted and warned about a future marked by escalating heat extremes. Two decades later, this projection has been robustly confirmed by observational evidence and climate model simulations. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) estimates with virtual certainty that hot extremes have become more frequent and more intense across most land regions since the 1950s, while cold extremes have become less frequent and less severe due to human-induced climate change.

With record-breaking temperatures and the intensity and impact of extremes very likely to increase, strengthening national and international early warnings and anticipatory action to reduce the impacts on human health, livelihood and the environment is strongly needed.

Climate Change and Heatwaves

Until the late twentieth century, heatwaves were largely regarded as recurrent meteorological phenomena. They were generally considered to be mostly independent of human influence and as difficult to predict as other natural hazards such as earthquakes.  More recent research instead attributes the increased likelihood and intensity of 213 historical heatwaves observed between 2000 and 2023 to anthropogenic climate change, showing that each of the 180 major fossil fuel and cement producers (“carbon majors”) made a substantial contribution.

The IPCC Working Group I report, Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis, found that reaching 1.5°C of global warming will cause more frequent heatwaves, longer warm seasons, and shorter cold seasons. At 2°C of global warming, heat extremes would more often reach critical tolerance thresholds for agriculture and health.

These findings were subsequently consolidated and expanded in the AR6 Synthesis Report published in March 2023, which concludes with high confidence that human-induced climate change has increased both the frequency and intensity of heatwaves since the 1950s. The Synthesis Report further emphasizes that continued warming will amplify these trends and that human influence has likely increased the frequency of compound extreme events, including simultaneous heatwaves and droughts at the global scale. Each additional 0.5°C of warming leads to clearly observable increases in the intensity, frequency and duration of temperature extremes, and thus heatwaves. 

Health Impacts of Heatwaves

The World Health Organization cites heat stress as the leading cause of weather-related deaths: it can exacerbate underlying conditions such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, asthma, mental health disorders, increase the risk of accidents and the transmission of certain infectious diseases. Heatstroke is a medical emergency with a high case-fatality rate. Heatwaves negatively affect key bodily functions such as thermoregulation and kidney processes, leading to increased illness and death. Certain population groups are especially vulnerable to heat effects, even in regions with moderate temperatures. These include older adults (65+), infants and children, whose bodies struggle to adapt to sudden temperature changes, as well as people with chronic cardiovascular and respiratory conditions, obesity and those taking medications that impair heat regulation.

The number of people exposed to extreme heat is increasing exponentially due to climate change in all regions of the world, with heat-related mortality among people over the age of 65 having increased by approximately 85% between 2000–2004 and 2017–2021. A study found that from 2000–2019 approximately 489 000 heat-related deaths occurred each year, including more than 70 000 who died during the 2003 heatwave in Europe. Between 2000 and 2016, the number of people exposed to heatwaves increased by around 125 million.

The negative health impacts of heat are predictable and largely preventable through targeted public health and multisectoral policies and interventions. Tackling climate change, combined with comprehensive risk preparedness and management, can save lives today and in the future. Useful resources on how to manage and minimize health risks related to heatwaves include:

  • Global Heath Health Information Network
  • Beat the Heat Initiative against extreme heat and related environmental health risks in workplaces and major events| WHO
  • Tips for keeping cool| WHO
  • Extreme Heat Risk Management Scorecard| UNDRR
  • Climate change and workplace heat stress: technical report and guidance| WHO & WMO | 22 August 2025
  • Heatwaves and Health: Guidance on Warning-System Development| WHO & WMO | 2015

Environmental Impacts of Heatwaves

“Heat waves are among the most frequent and deadly impacts of climate change, along with floods and shrinking ice cover,” said Inger Andersen, Executive Director of UNEP. “We must be prepared for the risks these impacts pose, especially for society’s most vulnerable, including older persons. Yet as 7th edition of the Frontiers Report, The Weight of Time – Facing a new age of challenges for people and ecosystems shows, solutions exist that can help protect communities and restore ecosystems long-thought to have been lost.”

Vegetation and Wildlife

Heatwaves, together with droughts, are also associated with fire weather, related to high temperatures, low soil moisture, and low humidity, which in turn negatively affects vegetation and wildlife.  A study published in the Environmental and Science Policy journal in 2025 reveals that heatwaves significantly impact wildlife feed quality, reproductivity, and mortality. Vegetation is equally affected, as increased heat impacts the water and carbon balances of vegetation in different ways. In general, flora is heavily compromised regarding photosynthesis and gross primary production rates. Current and expected heat-driven losses of vegetation, natural cover and wildlife highlight the need for monitoring and swift management actions to safeguard wildlife and restore vegetation.  → WWF Ways to Help Wildlife in the Heat

Marine Life and Freshwater Systems

Marine heatwaves, a consequence of the increased warming of seas and the ocean, have also been rising in recent years,  contributing to the likelihood and strength of extreme weather events such as tropical storms and hurricanes, and disrupting the water cycle; making floods, droughts and wildfires on land more likely. The increasing frequency of marine heatwaves also impacts water resources. leaving marine species little time to recover, driving some toward extinction.

Other Impacts of Heatwaves

Cities and Heatwaves

People living in cities and towns tend to be the hardest hit because urban areas are generally hotter than the surrounding countryside.

Rapid urbanization and the urban heat island effect are causing cities to heat up at twice the global average rate. Climate change-induced rising temperatures aggravate urban heating and make people living in urban areas more exposed to heat stress and the resulting health risks compared to people living in non-urban areas.

Cities are also driving solutions to prevent, prepare for, and minimize the impact of heatwaves, with more and more cities developing action plans or implementing innovative measures to protect their residents from heat exhaustion. To successfully do so, the involvement of various governmental departments, civil society and other actors is necessary and must be coupled with a combination of long-term planning and seasonal and short-term considerations (IFRC, 2020):

  • Incorporate funding for heat action into municipal budgets.
  • Outlining the mandate to reduce heat risks within municipal policies and laws;
  • working with national stakeholders to ensure heatwaves are eligible for humanitarian financing in national disaster management laws;
  • integrating heat action throughout city planning decisions;
  • and encouraging the private sector and non-governmental partners to develop complementary heat action plans.

Additional resources:

  • Ten Essentials for Making Cities Resilient| UNDRR
  • Managing heat-health risks in warming cities: Governance innovations and gaps from Geneva, Switzerland| Nicole Swaney, Anna Scolobig, Markus Stoffel | Progress in Disaster Science | April 2026
  • Charted: How Could Rising Temperatures Affect Your City?| World Resources Institute | 26 March 2026
  • Heat resilience starts at the local level| Global Cities Hub | 2 June 2025
  • Boiling hot: how cities can beat the growing threat of extreme heat| UNEP | 25 July 2025
  • Extreme heat is changing the way we live. This is how| WEF | 25 March 2025
  • Rising temperatures, rising tensions: The urban climate-conflict nexus amid the global housing crisis| UN-Habitat | 2025
  • Impact of heatwaves on all-cause mortality in India: a comprehensive multi-city study| Environment International | 26 January 2024
  • Heat Resilient Cities: Measuring benefits of urban heat adaptation| C40 | March 2021
  • Heat wave guide for cities| IFRC | 20 November 2020

UN Secretary-General Call to Action on Extreme Heat

In response to the rapid rise in the scale, intensity, frequency and duration of extreme heat, UN Secretary-General António Guterres on 25 July 2024 called for an urgent and concerted effort to enhance international cooperation. Developed in collaboration with 10 UN agencies, the call aims to address extreme heat in four critical areas: Caring for the vulnerable, protecting workers, boosting resilience of economies and societies using data and science and limiting temperature rise to 1.5°C by phasing out fossil fuels and scaling up investment in renewable energy. 

Cooling Efforts

While cooling is an immediate solution to relieve heat stress, ensure food safety, and sustain economic productivity, its impact on emissions is non-negligible: as underscored by UNEP, we cannot just air condition ourselves out of this crisis. Today, cooling accounts for a fifth of global electricity consumption and is a top driver of new power generation capacity. Without urgent action, cooling-related emissions could more than double by 2050, reaching up to 6.1 billion tons of CO₂e and representing over 10% of global emissions. At the same time, around 1.2 billion people still lack access to vital cooling services, impacting vulnerable populations and risking lives through food spoilage and vaccine loss. Delivering sustainable cooling can slash emissions, save trillions in energy costs, and drive progress across the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

To transition to more sustainable and equitable ways to cool down and ensure access to cooling where needed, without further warming the planet, UNEP Cool Coalition, was launched as a flagship initiative of the UN Secretary-General’s Climate Action Summit in 2019. This global platform brings together over 300 governments, businesses, cities, and financial institutions to drive comprehensive action on sustainable cooling and extreme heat. The Coalition advances implementation of the Paris Agreement, the 2030 Agenda, and the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol by working across three pillars: advocacy, science, and joint action. It champions a holistic approach to cooling and cold chains in both industrialized and developing countries, promoting passive cooling (i.e. cool roofs, nature-based solutions, better building and urban design), improved energy efficiency, and the rapid phase-down of climate-warming refrigerants. The Cool Coalition also serves as the Secretariat to the Global Cooling Pledge.

Other efforts to advance sustainable cooling include:

  • Beat the Heat/Mutirão contra o Calor Extremo| COP30 – UNEP Cool Coalition flagship implementation drive
  • Global Cooling Pledge for COP28, signed by 71 parties.
  • Global Cooling Watch 2025| UNEP | 11 November 2025
  • Global Cooling Watch 2023| UNEP | 5 December 2023

Heat Action Day

Launched by the IFRC in 2022, Heat Action Day is observed on 2 June to raise awareness and action on extreme heat – an increasingly deadly threat worldwide.

2026 Theme: The particular risks of indoor heat

On 2 June 2026, the 5th edition of Heat Action Day (HAD) will bring communities together worldwide to tackle the growing threat of extreme heat. This year’s theme — indoor heat — puts the spotlight on the health risks people face inside their homes, schools, workplaces, and care facilities. Whether you’re sharing life-saving tips on social media or organizing a community event, there are many ways to get involved and help #BeatTheHeat.

  • Cities will light up their public landmarks in orange to mark Heat Action Day
  • Red Cross and Red Crescent National Societies and partner organizations create events, skits, plays, games, etc. to demonstrate how to recognize the signs of heat exhaustion and heatstroke in public places, and how to help.
  • Engage experts, media, movers and shakers to spread the word about recognizing the signs of, preventing and treating heat exhaustion and heat stroke.
  • In advance of Heat Action Day, we are encouraging people around the world to send in cartoons, drawings, paintings, data visualizations related to heat exhaustion and heat stroke. IFRC will create an online project booklet from this to be shared widely.

Role of Geneva

International Actors

Organizations are listed in alphabetical order.

Global Cities Hub – Geneva

The Global Cities Hub is at the forefront of linking Local and Regional Governments and international organizations to address the most pressing challenges faced by urban centers worldwide. Working on critical areas such as environment and climate change, smart city development, protection of human rights, and promotion of urban health, the Global Cities Hub highlights the challenges, solutions and best practices LRGs and cities from across the world take to beat the heat.

Group on Earth Observations | GEO

GEO makes available Earth observations in support of effective policy responses for climate change adaptation, mitigation and other specific provisions, working with partners to enhance global observation systems for climate action. The Global Heat Resilience Service (GHRS) is GEO’s contribution to the UN Secretary-General’s Call to Action on Extreme Heat,  aiming to close critical knowledge and action gaps by providing cities with a comprehensive approach to heat resilience. Through standardized methodologies and integrated data services, GHRS helps identify urban “hot spots” and vulnerable populations, accounting for factors such as healthcare access, infrastructure quality, and socioeconomic status. These insights are translated into actionable intelligence through user-friendly digital tools that help cities make the case for action and guide investment decisions. GHRS then supports implementation through policy frameworks and community engagement, ensuring solutions are locally appropriate—particularly vital for low- and middle-income cities.

Impact Initiatives

Impact Initiatives is a leading Geneva-based think-and-do tank that aims to improve the impact of humanitarian, stabilization and development action through data, partnerships and capacity-building programmes. Through its REACH initiative, Impact Initiatives provides granular data, timely information and in-depth analysis from contexts of crisis, disaster and displacement. The work of REACH directly feeds into aid response and decision-making by providing accessible and precise information on the humanitarian situation of crisis-affected populations.

Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change | IPCC

The IPCC was set up to provide an objective source of scientific information. In its Sixth Assessment Report, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) reported that human-caused climate change has increased the frequency and intensity of heatwaves since the 1950s and additional warming will further increase their frequency and intensity.

International Federation of the Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies | IFRC

The International Federation of the Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) is the world’s largest humanitarian network. As part of its mission to reduce risks and address disasters and health emergencies to meet the needs and improve the lives of vulnerable people, IFRC engages in various campaigns on the risks related to heathwaves. Launched by the IFRC in 2022, Heat Action Day is observed on 2 June to raise awareness and action on extreme heat – an increasingly deadly threat worldwide.

International Institute for Sustainable Development | IISD

IISD is actively involved in the two main responses to climate change: adaptation and mitigation. It partners with countries to help them cope with a changing climate and transition to clean energy as quickly as possible. IISD is engaged to help decision-makers devise policies and programs that will be sustainable over the long-term, including through nature-based solutions.

International Labour Organization | ILO

The International Labour Organization (ILO) is actively addressing heatwaves through international guidelines, research and the promotion of occupational safety and health (OSH) policies to protect workers. This includes analyses of national legislation on heat stress in 21 countries, recommendations for workplace measures during heatwaves, and reports on the impacts of heat stress on labour productivity and decent work.

International Organization on Migration | IOM

The International Organization for Migration (IOM), as the lead convening actor on climate mobility, works across six continents with Member States and partners to address climate-related drivers of displacement, including extreme heat. Since 2007, IOM Member States have mandated the Organization to develop solutions for people on the move in a changing climate, which was formalized in 2015 with the creation of the first UN global team dedicated to climate migration. In response to the escalating climate crisis and the growing role of heatwaves in shaping human mobility, IOM launched its 10-year Institutional Strategy on Migration, Environment and Climate Change (2021–2030). The Strategy integrates heat-related mobility into policy and operational action, with the goal of reducing vulnerability, protecting rights and empowering affected populations. Its implementation is guided by the Climate Mobility Road Map, which promotes people-centered solutions that respect individuals’ freedom to choose whether to stay or migrate. However, the scale of the challenge is significant: new analysis from IOM’s Global Data Institute estimates that up to 2.8 billion people could be exposed to heatwaves by 2090 under a high-warming scenario.

Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change | IPCC

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) is the United Nations body for assessing the science related to climate change. Through its assessment reports and special reports, the IPCC divulgates objective scientific information on the state of scientific, technical and socio-economic knowledge on climate change, its impacts and future risks, and options for reducing the rate at which climate change is taking place.  The Working Group I contribution to the Sixth Assessment Report “Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis” dedicated its eleventh chapter to Weather and Climate Extreme Events in a Changing Climate, establishing that human-induced greenhouse gas emissions have led to an increased frequency and/or intensity of some weather and climate extremes since pre-industrial time, in particular for temperature extremes

International Telecommunication Union | ITU

The International Telecommunication Union (ITU) works on addressing climate change through information and communication technologies (ICTs). Work includes supporting the growth of satellite surveillance and monitoring services from space for, for example, more accurate weather forecasting and prediction of extreme weather events linked to climate change, and particularly for predicting the strength, path and location of landfall of tropical storms. The ITU is a key partner in the United Nations’ Early Warnings for All (EW4All) initiative, focusing specifically on the dissemination and communication of heatwave alerts. As heatwaves become more frequent and severe due to climate change, the ITU works to ensure that by 2027, everyone on Earth is protected by and has access to early warning systems that can reduce potential damage from extreme heat by 30%.

Office of the High Commissioner on Human Rights | OHCHR

The Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) and UN human rights officials have addressed heatwaves as a significant threat to human rights. They categorize extreme heat as a “silent killer” that violates rights to life, health, and a healthy environment, particularly for vulnerable populations. Over the past years, the Human Rights Council took on resolutions and discussions on specific aspects of climate change, while Special Rapporteurs contributed with reports on specific thematic angles within their mandates. The Human Rights Council has contributed to raising awareness of the links between human rights and climate change by successive and targeted clarifications of the ways climate change affects human rights, including through the adoption of a series of resolutions related to climate change and human rights.

International Union for Conservation of Nature | IUCN

The IUCN monitors the impacts of climate change on nature and guides the conservation and restoration of ecosystems to help mitigate and adapt to it. More specifically, it has focused on the impact of marine heatwaves and provides key information on how to address this critical issue, due to its severe and long-lasting consequences on marine and human life.

Partnership for Environment and Disaster Risk Reduction | PEDRR

PEDRR is the clearinghouse for knowledge, training, advocacy and practice on Ecosystem-based Disaster Risk Reduction (Eco-DRR). Eco-DRR is the sustainable management, conservation and restoration of ecosystems to reduce disaster risk, with the aim to achieve sustainable and resilient development. Well-managed ecosystems, such as wetlands, forests and coastal systems, act as natural infrastructure, reducing physical exposure to many hazards and increasing socio-economic resilience of people and communities by sustaining local livelihoods and providing essential natural resources such as food, water and building materials.

Platform on Disaster Replacement

Climate refugees are on the increase and the Platform on Disaster Displacement is a State-led initiative that aims to follow up on the work started by the Nansen Initiative and to implement the Nansen Initiative – Agenda for the Protection of Cross-Border Displaced Persons in the Context of Disasters and Climate Change, endorsed by more than 100 States in October 2015.

United Nations Economic Commission for Europe | UNECE

The UNECE region is a major source of greenhouse gas emissions, responsible for 34% of the world’s CO2 emissions from fossil fuel combustion. The people of the region are increasingly feeling the impacts of climate change – from wildfires to flooding, heatwaves and drought. Through its norms, standards, conventions and policy assistance, UNECE provides key tools supporting countries in their climate change mitigation and adaptation efforts.

United Nations Human Settlements Programme | UN-Habitat

The United Nations Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat) is mandated by the UN General Assembly to promote socially and environmentally sustainable towns and cities. UN-Habitat advances urban resilience to extreme heat through climate-smart planning, improved housing and infrastructure design, nature-based solutions, and community-led heat action plans, laying the groundwork for cities that are cooler, safer, and more inclusive.

United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees | UNHCR

Refugees, internally displaced and stateless people are on the frontlines of the climate crisis. UNHCR is working to protect them and strengthen their resilience to its current and future impacts, while also reducing our own environmental footprint. In its recent report “No Escape II – The way forward. Bringing climate solutions to the frontlines of conflict and displacement“, UNHCR has alerted that nearly all current refugee settlements will face an unprecedented rise in hazardous heat. By 2050, the hottest fifteen refugee camps in the world – located in Gambia, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Senegal and Mali – are projected to face nearly 200 days or more of hazardous heat stress per year.

United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction | UNDRR

The United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR) is the UN agency engaged in reducing the risks posed by disasters by supporting governments, and decision-makers to adopt better policies and invest in DRR, as well as collect, collate, and share the latest high-quality technical information and data about reducing risk and building resilience more effectively. UNDRR works to strengthen integrated extreme heat risk governance, including through the Extreme Heat Decision-Support Package, developed with the Global Heat Health Information Network, WMO and Duke University’s Heat Policy Innovation Hub. UNDRR also developed the Extreme Heat Risk Management Scorecard to governments on the sub-national and local levels in assessing and strengthening their efforts for heat risk management.

World Intellectual Property Office | WIPO

The World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) advances action on heatwaves through WIPO GREEN, a marketplace and database for sustainable technology, which actively promotes solutions for adapting to extreme temperatures. WIPO’s Green Technology Book series also provides information on recent technologies that can aid in tackling some of the most critical environmental challenges of our time. The latest edition looks at solutions for confronting climate disasters, highlighting how innovation is transforming disaster preparedness, response, and recovery. The latter has a dedicated chapter to extreme temperatures, including extreme heat, showcasing how innovative cooling technologies help combat extreme heat through urban and infrastructure solutions.

World Bank Group Geneva Office

The World Bank Group is the biggest multilateral funder of climate investments in developing countries and a global leader in disaster risk management, supporting countries to assess exposure to hazards and address disaster risks.

World Economic Forum | WEF

The World Economic Forum works at the intersection of geopolitics and cooperation, believing that the only viable path forward is to connect leaders across sectors, regions, ideologies and generations to make sense of global challenges and move the world forward together. In 2025, it developed a roadmap, “Insuring Against Extreme Heat:
Navigating Risks in a Warming World” for addressing the impacts and causes of extreme heat is a multifaceted framework with specific objectives.

World Health Organization | WHO

The World Health Organization, as the global health authority, supports countries in building resilient and proactive health systems that can anticipate the needs and challenges during emergencies so that they are more likely to reduce risks and respond effectively when needed, monitor health hazards and impacts of events and provide guidelines. The WHO is actively involved in mitigating the health risks of heatwaves through guidance, policy, and partnerships. WHO also collaborates with other UN agencies and other international experts to work together for a world where extreme heat is no barrier to lives and livelihoods. This includes the Global Heat Health Information Network, co-led with the World Meteorological Organization.

World Meteorological Organization | WMO

The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) promotes international cooperation to improve the monitoring, characterization and sharing of heatwave information, including the verification of record-breaking temperatures. Working closely with its National Meteorological and Hydrological Services (NMHS), WMO strengthens heatwave prediction capabilities and supports the modernization of early warning systems for extreme heat. These efforts enable countries to coordinate heat action plans and translate warnings into effective action at the community level.

In collaboration with the World Health Organization (WHO), WMO has developed heat–health warning guidance (WMO-No.1142), providing practical tools to address the health impacts of extreme heat and heatwaves. WMO also co-sponsors the Global Heat Health Information Network, which serves as a key platform for partnership, knowledge exchange and user-driven collaboration on heat-related risks. More information on the WMO’s work related to heatwaves can be found here.

Local Geneva

actif-trafiC

actif-trafiC is an independent association supported by nearly 65,000 people and committed to promoting sustainable mobility. During the heatwave of July 2022, actif-trafiC carried out surface temperature measurements at several locations and observed the vital importance of vegetation in cooling the city, as called for by the Urban Climate initiatives launched in several Swiss cities.

Canton of Geneva

The canton of Geneva has a dedicated webpage providing recommendations for heatwave situations. The information is intended for professionals and municipalities, and includes contact details for the Cantonal Medical Service, as well as campaign materials for informing the general public. 

Climate Action Accelerator

Climate Action Accelerator is a not-for-profit initiative that aims to mobilise a critical mass of common good organisations to scale-up climate solutions, empowering them to radically transform their practices and contribute to tipping entire sectors of society towards low-carbon, resilient and sustainable models.

City of Geneva

The City of Geneva is active in protecting its population from extreme climate events, especially heatwaves. Since 2014, the heatwave plans (Plans Canicule) offer various measures to protect the most vulnerable to extreme temperatures in summer, namely refreshing areas, shaded refuges from heat islands, free swimming pools, and more.

Additional Resources

  • Global Heath Health Information Network
  • Extreme Heat Portal
  • Extreme Heat Risk Management Scorecard| UNDRR
  • Extreme Heat Decision-Support Package| Global Heath Health Information Network, WMO, UNDRR, Duke University
  • Heatwaves| WHO
  • Rethinking how buildings can adapt to extreme heat| UNDRR | 8 May 2026
  • The hidden links between heat, water, and energy| UNDRR | 8 May 2026
  • Three billion urban residents can avoid unprecedented future heat| Hamish Lewis & Luke Harrington | Climate Change | 18 April 2026
  • Extreme Heat Risk Governance Framework and Toolkit| Global Heath Health Information Network, WMO, UNDRR, Duke University | 2025
  • Heat, air pollution and solar ultraviolet radiation: mass gathering-specific considerations and research gaps| WHO | 25 December 2025
  • UNHCR report reveals extreme weather driving repeated displacement among conflict-affected communities| UNHCR | 10 November 2025
  • Extreme heat can raise newborn death risk by 22 percent| Vox.com | 13 August 2025
  • Extreme heat impacts millions of people| WMO | 7 August 2025
  • Heatwaves and wildfires coincide with anniversary of Call to Action on Extreme Heat| WMO | 28 July 2025
  • Heat waves put older persons at high risk, warns UN report| UNEP | 10 July 2025
  • ARTE Europe Weekly : How heatwaves expose inequality| UNIGE | 9 July 2025
  • Climate change tripled heat-related deaths in early summer European heatwave| Imperial College Grantham Institute – Climate Change and the Environment | 2 July 2025
  • Extreme weather in a changing climate: Is Europe prepared?| European Environment Agency | 2 July 2025
  • Northern hemisphere heatwave underscores value of early-warning alerts| UN News | 1 July 2025
  • Heat: Through the eyes of the most vulnerable-Perceptions and pathways to action| IFRC and Red Cross Red Crescent Climate Centre | 1 June 2025
  • Frontiers 2025: The Weight of Time – Facing a new age of challenges for people and ecosystems| UNEP | 10 July 2025
  • The silent killer: We need better risk governance to beat extreme heat | GP 2025| UNDRR | 11 June 2025
  • The multidimensional impacts of heatwaves on human ecosystems: A systematic literature review and future research direction| Matthew Abunyewah, Thayaparan Gajendran, Michael Odei Erdiaw-Kwasie et al | Environmental Science & Policy | March 2025
  • COP29 Special Report on Climate Change and Health| WHO | 7 November 2024
  • Lancet Countdown on health and climate change: facing record-breaking threats from delayed action| Lancet | 29 October 2024
  • Up to 2.8 Billion People Possibly Exposed to Heatwaves Worldwide by 2090: New IOM Analysis| IOM | 5 December 2023
  • Launch of global indicator of outdoor workers in the Lancet Countdown on Health and Climate Change| WHO | 16 November 2023
  • Climate change and heatwaves| WMO | 21 September 2023
  • Change in cooling degree days with global mean temperature rise increasing from 1.5 °C to 2.0 °C
    | Nicole D. Miranda, Jesus Lizana, Sarah N. Sparrow et al. | Nature Sustainability | 13 July 2023
  • Heat-related mortality in Europe during the summer of 2022| Nature Medicine | 10 July 2023
  • WMO Bulletin: heatwaves worsen air quality and pollution| WMO | 6 September 2023
  • Extreme heat: Preparing for the heat waves of the future| OCHA and IFRC | 10 October 2022
  • Protecting People from Extreme Heat | 10 Steps for Governments to Address Human Rights Impact of Climate Crisis | 21 July 2022
  • Hot weather and heat extremes: health risks| The Lancet | August 2021
  • Western North American extreme heat virtually impossible without human-caused climate change| World Weather Attribution | 7 July 2021
  • Rapid attribution analysis of the extraordinary heatwave on the Pacific Coast of the US and Canada June 2021| Sjoukje Y. Philip, Sarah F. Kew, Geert Jan van Oldenborgh et al. | 2021
  • Extreme Heat and Migration| IOM | 2017
  • Attributing human mortality during extreme heat waves to anthropogenic climate change| Mitchell, Heaviside, Vardoulakis et al. | Environment Research Letters | 8 July 2016
